episodes.title: S&P to 8,000 This Year?
episodes.summary:
**Summary**
Goldman's head of Americas equity sales trading lays out a constructive S&P 500 bull case targeting 8,000, anchored in record earnings momentum and a broadening buyback bid. The single most important risk flagged for EM traders: markets are currently pricing ~40 bps of Fed hikes by year-end following a more hawkish-than-expected Warsh-led June FOMC — Goldman's own economists do not expect a hike, and a hold would function as an effective ease, which is the key binary for USD/EM dynamics.

**Key points**
- June FOMC (Warsh's first meeting as chair) characterised as more hawkish than expected; rates market pricing ~40 bps of hikes by year-end
- Goldman economists do not expect any hike; a hold = effective cut in market perception
- Two June IPOs totalled $140 bn notional — first and second largest primary capital raises in US history within a two-week window; retail demand accelerating
- Record single-day volume: 34 bn shares traded on one session, surpassing Liberation Day 2025
- Corporate buybacks broadening materially — 50–60 active programs/day on Goldman's desk vs. ~10 two years ago; record repurchase year expected even with some Mag Seven pauses
- Semis and semi-equipment the dominant crowded momentum trade; Korea and Taiwan cited as the primary equity expression
- Near-term headwind: pension rebalancing estimated at ~$30 bn in US equity sales over June 29–30
- Q1 median EPS +14% YoY; Q2 consensus at +9% YoY, seen as achievable

**FX / rates implications**
- Hawkish Fed repricing (40 bps hikes priced) is the core USD-positive risk for EM — if Goldman's no-hike base case holds, a hold-as-cut dynamic would compress USD/DXY and support INR, IDR, MXN, BRL carry
- Korea and Taiwan flagged as high-concentration equity plays in semis; crowded positioning creates tail risk for KRW and TWD on any semis de-rating or profit-taking
- $30 bn end-of-month pension equity selling could briefly lift UST demand, nudging yields lower — a marginal positive for EM duration heading into July
- Continued S&P strength and record institutional/retail bid sustains the risk-on backdrop underpinning EM carry, but semi crowding is an idiosyncratic volatility source in KRW and TWD

**Watch list**
- Fed hold vs. hike: 40 bps priced in by year-end — any labour/CPI data shifting that pricing is the dominant USD/EM catalyst
- Pension rebalancing flows June 29–30: potential near-term US equity soft patch and UST bid
- Q2 US earnings season (early July): 9% YoY consensus is the hurdle; beat or miss sets tone for H2 risk appetite and EM flows
- Semis positioning in KRW/TWD: monitor for squeeze or unwind as hedge funds cited as short Mag Seven to fund semi exposure

episodes.title: 265: Inflation and Investing: What Sticky Prices Mean for Portfolios Today - Ask Me Anything
episodes.summary:
**Summary**
BlackRock's Global TAA senior PM and International Equities CIO argue that AI infrastructure capex, fiscal expansion, and Middle East supply constraints have structurally re-anchored inflation above target across Western economies, validating a short-duration bias since 2021. For EM FX/rates traders the key transmission is higher-for-longer Fed and ECB, sustained USD firmness, and commodity price floors — all headwinds for rate-sensitive EM carry.

**Key points**
- US tech sector guiding ~$800 bn in AI capex this year, driving excess demand in electricity, copper, chips and construction; global electricity demand growth from AI equivalent to adding a Japan per year
- ~80 million barrels of crude and refined products reported trapped in the Persian Gulf at time of recording; Middle East supply constraints lifting oil and broader commodity prices
- ECB renewed its hiking cycle at time of recording; Fed rate cuts priced only toward year-end — five-plus years of consecutive above-target inflation misses across Western central banks
- China flagged explicitly as a deflationary outlier: closed consumption basket and housing market drag have kept it in a low/negative inflation regime, contrasting sharply with US and Europe
- Stock-bond correlation turns positive when inflation exceeds target, undermining 60/40 diversification; BlackRock has held short nominal duration since 2021
- Pricing power concentrated in energy, AI infrastructure, and commodities (copper, gas); food and consumer-staple companies squeezed by input costs with no pass-through ability

**FX / rates implications**
- Sticky US inflation plus delayed Fed cuts keeps DXY supported — broad headwind for EM Asia and LATAM high-beta FX (MXN, BRL, IDR, PHP)
- Persistent oil and copper supply constraints provide a floor for commodity-linked EM currencies: CLP and PEN (copper/metals), COP and MXN (oil)
- China's deflationary divergence limits aggressive PBoC easing; CNY stability maintained but no strong upside catalyst from domestic demand
- ECB resuming hikes compresses EUR carry drag versus EM, marginally supportive for EM carry trades funded in EUR

**Watch list**
- US CPI/PCE/PPI prints: any upside surprise pushes Fed cuts further out, reinforcing USD strength
- Persian Gulf crude situation: escalation risks an oil spike that widens EM commodity divergence
- LME copper: proxy for AI infrastructure demand intensity and a direct signal for CLP and PEN

episodes.title: Looking beyond Hormuz
episodes.summary:
**Summary**
HSBC Global Research's Janet Henry and Ali Chakaroblu review the macro landscape post-Strait of Hormuz reopening, dissect their proprietary EM investor sentiment survey, and spell out a divergent EM central bank outlook with direct implications for Asia FX and LATAM rates. The episode is dense with actionable reads for EM FX/rates — local currency bonds severely underweighted, dollar cited as the top investor risk, and HSBC flagging residual tightening pressure in India, Indonesia and the Philippines while Brazil pivots to cuts in 2027.

**Key points**
- Strait of Hormuz gradually reopening after US-Iran deal; oil back to end-February levels, removing the worst upside inflation shock, but lagged effects on sulfur, fertilizer and food prices persist for months
- HSBC EM sentiment survey: net bullish still elevated at 60% (bulls 60%, bears absent for third consecutive survey), but net positioning declined across all EM assets — EM equities net long collapsed from 57% to 35%; local currency bonds now severely underweight at net minus 32%
- USD identified as the number-one downside risk by survey respondents; capital reallocation out of the US into EM cited as the top upside catalyst
- More than 80% of survey respondents expect EM inflation to accelerate over the next 12 months, sharply higher vs the March survey; growth expectations moderated but still positive
- HSBC revised 2027 growth down for India and Brazil on Hormuz carryover and El Niño exposure; El Niño flagged as a potential super/severe event with outsized food-price impact on EM Asia and Brazil
- Inflation not seen falling materially until Q2 next year even in the base case; no second-round effects required for that outcome
- ECB: June hike likely the last; HSBC base case is a rate cut in H2 this year. BoE: may avoid hiking altogether, cuts possible in 2026. Fed: on hold through 2026, no cuts priced by HSBC, but close call on labour market
- 56% of EM survey respondents expect EM policy rates to rise over the next three months

**FX / rates implications**
- Asia local rates under pressure: HSBC explicitly flags residual tightening risk in India (RBI), Indonesia (BI) and the Philippines (BSP) if USD stays firm and Asian currencies remain under pressure — INR, IDR, PHP all vulnerable to a hawkish repricing
- LATAM divergence: Brazil (BRL) expected to resume cutting in 2027 after an extended pause, aided by lower oil and El Niño resolution — but near-term BRL sensitive to food/inflation surprise and still-tight BCB stance
- KRW and TWD supported by AI/tech export boom (Taiwan Q1 GDP +14% YoY, ~80% driven by tech exports; Korea equity inflows picking up) — positioning shift into Asia equities has FX spillover
- Local currency EM bond market net minus 32% positioning is a significant underweight; any durable oil decline and Fed-on-hold confirmation could trigger a sharp squeeze/cover rally in EM local rates
- USD/DXY remains the swing factor: dollar strength was cited as the top investor concern; easing DXY would unlock the capital reallocation catalyst and broadly supportive for EM FX carry
- El Niño food inflation risk is an idiosyncratic negative for INR, IDR, PHP and BRL — central banks in these jurisdictions may be forced to stay on hold or tighten even as global rates ease

**Watch list**
- Strait of Hormuz reopening pace and sustainability — a durable full reopening is the single largest upside catalyst across oil, food, and EM risk
- El Niño severity updates from climate agencies — super El Niño scenario would materially re-price food CPI across EM Asia and Brazil
- Fed labour market data (unemployment, NFP) — HSBC on hold, but a tightening labour market is the key risk to that call and would transmit directly into EM via USD
- ECB communication shift — HSBC expects H2 cut but the bank has not signalled it; watch for tone change at September meeting
- Next HSBC EM sentiment survey for trend in local currency bond positioning (currently at minus 32%) and regional rotation signals
- Oil price stability around 75-80 — key threshold below which EM central banks (especially Asia) could price out hikes and EM local bonds rally

episodes.title: Ep. 364: William Beach on the US Fiscal Precipice, Tax Reform, and AI's Impact on Labour
episodes.summary:
**Summary**
Former BLS Commissioner and fiscal-policy veteran William Beach outlines a deepening US fiscal crisis — crowding out, a reversed convenience yield, and interest costs consuming half of income-tax revenue by 2030-36 — with Social Security facing a statutory 28% benefit cut as early as 2035. For EM traders the read-through is via US Treasury supply, term premium, and the USD: a structurally heavy issuance calendar with deteriorating demand dynamics is the single biggest upward pressure on the long end.

**Key points**
- Convenience yield has turned negative: the market no longer accepts sub-market rates on Treasuries, forcing higher coupon costs and transmitting immediately into private-sector borrowing rates.
- Interest payments now exceed the US defence budget; Beach projects they will consume ~50% of all federal income-tax revenue by 2030-36.
- Social Security trust fund exhaustion projected 2035; with election-year blackouts, effective reform window is roughly two off-years — making a benefit cut or payroll-tax hike increasingly likely.
- Fiscal space estimated to ~2040-41 before a true debt spiral, but Beach's target is a 3% GDP deficit as a stabilisation anchor.
- US labour market characterised as broadening: JOLTS openings back at 7 million, positive monthly payroll revisions, FDI rising; inflation expectations subsiding is credited with unlocking business expansion.
- Tariff note: IEPA tariffs were declared unconstitutional (brief relief), but Trump administration is preparing an alternative approach — uncertainty remains.
- BLS data quality flagged: birth-death model systematically overshooting, household survey response rate down to 65% and falling; benchmark revisions have been large and negative, meaning recent payroll strength may be overstated.
- AI-labour complementarity rising in the tech sector; broader productivity gains not yet visible in aggregate data.

**FX / rates implications**
- Heavy and accelerating Treasury supply, combined with deteriorating convenience yield, is structurally bearish for UST duration — supportive of higher 10y yields which tighten EM financial conditions via rate differentials and carry.
- A higher-for-longer US rate environment reinforces USD strength (DXY bid), compressing EM carry appeal and pressuring high-beta EM FX (BRL, COP, IDR, PHP).
- Tariff uncertainty returning as IEPA successor policy is designed — any new tariff shock is risk-off for EM Asia exporters (KRW, TWD, MYR, VND) and commodity-linked LATAM (CLP, COP, PEN).
- BLS data-quality caveat: if payroll strength is partly a birth-death artefact, Fed pivot could come sooner than the market prices — a tail scenario that would be risk-on for EM carry.

**Watch list**
- Trump administration's replacement tariff framework — timing and scope critical for EM Asia exporters.
- Annual BLS benchmark revision (March): could deliver another large downward payroll revision, repricing Fed path.
- Congressional action on HR1 ("One Big Beautiful Bill") Medicaid cuts and Social Security — any signal of fiscal credibility narrows term premium; failure widens it.
- US 10y auction tails and bid-to-cover ratios as a real-time read on convenience yield erosion.

episodes.title: Rory Johnston on Why His $200 Oil Prediction Didn't Turn Out Right
episodes.summary:
**Summary**
Rory Johnston (Commodity Context) delivers a post-mortem on why Brent never sustained above $120 despite the Strait of Hormuz closure cutting ~13 million b/d of Gulf supply — the largest supply disruption in oil market history. The episode is directly relevant to EM traders via China's role as the dominant swing factor, acute energy stress across EM Asia, and the forward question of when China re-enters as a buyer.

**Key points**
- Hormuz closure removed ~13 mb/d net of known rerouting options (East-West pipeline, Emirate pipeline to Fujairah); cumulative volume shut in is ~1.3 billion barrels to date.
- China cut crude imports by ~5 mb/d (roughly half the spot market supply hit to Asia), absorbing the shock via a mix of refined product stockdraw, possible SPR releases from underground caverns (~131 mb known capacity), and modest demand substitution. Mobility data showed no COVID-zero-style demand destruction, leaving the exact mechanism opaque.
- IEA member SPR releases totalled ~400 mb at the upper end; China's import reduction was comparable or larger.
- South Korea, Japan, Australia, Taiwan faced acute supply stress; the Philippines introduced fuel-use restrictions. Those economies have since recovered to near pre-war import levels, facilitated by China not competing for barrels.
- Brent time spreads flipped to contango on June 24 — signal that the market is currently oversupplied as Hormuz reopens and trapped cargoes (~100+ mb) flush out, but China has not stepped back in.
- Trump administration's rhetoric and tariff uncertainty injected enough downside vol to blow out bullish prop traders' risk limits, amplifying the price cap effect.
- India is planning a ~50 mb strategic crude reserve; other countries that lacked SPRs pre-crisis are now building them, providing structural demand support over 1-3 years.
- Brent was ~$74 at recording (June 24, 2026), only modestly above pre-war ~$70; Johnston flags no firm floor given surplus conditions.

**FX / rates implications**
- Brent at $74 and in contango is a disinflationary tailwind for oil-importing EM Asia (INR, KRW, PHP, TWD, THB, IDR) — lower import bills ease current-account pressure and reduce CPI pass-through, giving central banks room to cut or hold without currency defense urgency.
- The critical unknown is China's re-entry timing: a 5 mb/d demand return would tighten spreads sharply, reversing the tailwind and pressuring energy-import EM currencies.
- India's SPR build adds structural crude demand, a mild long-run support for oil that could eventually weigh on INR via import costs.
- For MXN and BRL, sustained low Brent removes a commodity tailwind but also dampens inflation, a mixed signal for Banxico and BCB rate paths.

**Watch list**
- China crude import data (weekly tanker tracker, monthly customs): any uptick signals re-entry and could reprice Brent spreads from contango back to backwardation rapidly.
- Brent prompt spread (M1-M2): currently contango — flip back to backwardation would be the first sign of renewed tightness.
- Hormuz transit volumes and rerouting capacity: pace of cargo flush-out is unsustainably fast and itself a temporary supply source.
- India SPR procurement announcements: size and timing of the ~50 mb build will anchor a structural demand floor for Asian crude grades.

episodes.title: Credit Crunch: Around the World of Global Credit in 60 Minutes
episodes.summary:
**Summary**
Bloomberg Intelligence's mid-year credit strategy roundtable covers Asia, Middle East, Europe and US credit markets. For an EM FX/rates trader the key read-throughs are: PBoC constrained from easing by domestic inflation, stagflation risk building across Asian sovereigns, oil normalising lower on a Middle East MOU, and record-tight EM credit spreads leaving very thin break-evens if macro conditions deteriorate.

**Key points**
- Asia IG dollar spreads at all-time lows, break-even for positive excess returns in H2 is just 11 basis points — any spread widening back to 3-month-ago levels means losses.
- PBoC explicitly inflation-focused; strategists do not expect aggressive rate cuts and flag potential hikes if inflation overshoots, which would weigh on CNY dim sum returns.
- Asian stagflation risk flagged for H2 — rising domestic rates and deteriorating sovereign fiscal metrics across multiple EM Asia countries; default cycle possible in 2027.
- India dollar credit is the top-performing EM Asia market (+2% YTD), but the spread advantage has compressed 40 bp since January — break-even cushion eroding fast.
- China dollar issuance down 31-32% YoY (H1 2026 total ~$32.5 bn); dim sum market growing on the back of lower CNY rates vs elevated UST base rates.
- Middle East MOU: oil prices have retraced to pre-war levels; Gulf exports running at ~two-thirds of normal, suggesting markets had over-priced supply risk. Strait of Hormuz reopening cited as resuming gas flows.
- US IG heading for $1.75 tn gross issuance, driven by AI/hyper-scaler mega-deals (5 largest all AI-related, each >$20 bn); high UST base rates keep EM dollar funding costs elevated.
- Middle East peace deal fragility flagged as a tail risk — strategist warns markets are pricing in too much optimism; potential reversal in H2.

**FX / rates implications**
- PBoC on hold-to-tighten bias = CNY/CNH stable-to-firmer; limits rate-cut catalyst for Asia EM carry unwind risk.
- Oil to pre-war levels: negative for EM oil exporters (MXN, COP, BRL) if sustained; relief for oil importers (INR, IDR, PHP, THB) on current-account/inflation channel.
- Asian stagflation narrative = upward pressure on domestic rates across EM Asia, flatter-to-inverted rate curves possible; watch BSP, BI, RBI for policy response.
- High UST yields as issuance base rate constrain EM sovereign ability to tap dollar markets cheaply; India sovereign/quasi-sovereign funding costs remain elevated.
- Middle East reversal risk = potential risk-off spike in EM credit spreads, widening EM FX volatility.

**Watch list**
- Middle East MOU durability, especially post-US midterms — a reversal re-prices oil and GCC/EM sovereign risk.
- PBoC inflation prints: any overshoot accelerates the shift from easing to tightening bias, CNH implications.
- Strait of Hormuz gas flow normalisation — determines whether European chemical sector gains hold or reverse; broader Brent/WTI trajectory.
- India dollar credit spreads: if break-even cushion fully erodes, flows may rotate out, INR adjacent.
- US jobs market data: key input into stagflation scenario probability being modelled by BI rates team.

episodes.title: Through the noise: Deciphering Kevin Warsh
episodes.summary:
**Summary**
Standard Chartered CIO unpacks Kevin Warsh's hawkish debut as Fed chair, concludes the rhetoric is tactical rather than strategic — designed to tighten financial conditions now and reduce the probability of an actual rate hike later. For EM Asia traders the episode is rich with direct implications: oil at ~$70/bbl, JPY carry dynamics, and divergent Asian CB policy across INR/PHP/THB vs KRW/SGD.

**Key points**
- Warsh's first post-meeting briefing stressed the 2% inflation target, avoided forward guidance, and delivered a stronger USD plus weaker gold and equities — StanChart views this as intentional financial-conditions tightening via communication rather than policy action.
- Base case: Fed on hold through 2026. Low oil prices (~$70/bbl) cool headline CPI; labor market softening; AI productivity gains dampen wage-driven services inflation; political constraints (Trump calling for cuts) reduce likelihood of hikes.
- Key risk: stronger-than-expected growth and job creation could still force the Fed's hand; in that scenario USD stays supported but earnings cushion equities.
- USDJPY: BoJ hiking but US-Japan rate differential remains wide in absolute terms, sustaining yen-funded carry trades. Pair is technically overbought near 162 major resistance; intervention risk elevated. StanChart sees correction toward 156 (200-DMA).
- Oil at $70/bbl benefits Asian net energy importers: India, Thailand, Philippines see easing inflation, reduced external balance pressures, and greater CB room to support growth — bullish for local-currency bonds in those markets.
- South Korea and Singapore face demand-driven, AI-boom-linked inflation — CBs stay on hold longer, creating clear intra-Asia policy divergence.
- Tail risk: El Niño in Q4 could disrupt food supply and push energy prices back up across the region.
- AUD bonds: tactically bullish on FX-unhedged basis; RBA stays relatively hawkish on sticky core CPI; AUD underpinned by resilient domestic growth and stabilizing Chinese demand for Australian exports.

**FX / rates implications**
- INR, PHP, THB: lower oil reduces import bills and external financing pressures, giving RBI, BSP and BoT more room to ease — supportive of local-currency bond longs and mild currency appreciation bias.
- KRW, SGD: AI-driven demand inflation means BoK and MAS stay restrictive longer than peers; less room for rate cuts limits bond upside relative to INR/PHP.
- JPY: correction from ~162 toward 156 is StanChart's near-term call; watch for MoF/BoJ intervention rhetoric as the catalyst. Carry trades funded in JPY remain structurally intact while the US-Japan differential is wide.
- USD/DXY: Fed pause eventually caps dollar upside; Warsh hawkishness is communication-driven, not rate-path-driven — a ceiling for DXY rather than a sustained bull run.
- Gold: should recover once the market prices out any residual hike premium; near-term pressure while Warsh's messaging tilts hawkish.
- AXJ local bonds: oil disinflation + Fed pause = constructive backdrop; India, Philippines, Thailand are preferred markets.

**Watch list**
- USDJPY 162 resistance; downside target 156 (200-DMA); MoF intervention signals
- US CPI prints — key test of StanChart's Fed-on-hold thesis
- BoJ next meeting: market pricing another +25bp hike this year
- Oil price trajectory: $70/bbl is the pivot; any rebound recouples Asian inflation and CB restraint
- El Niño monitoring Q4: food and energy price shock risk for India, Philippines, Thailand
- Kevin Warsh speeches and any shift away from hawkish communication tone
- Korea/Taiwan semiconductor earnings as proxy for AXJ tech earnings concentration risk

episodes.title: The Week Ahead - The Second Wave
episodes.summary:
**Summary**
Nomura's weekly covers the full macro stack relevant to EM: the Fed hawkishness debate post-FOMC, ECB Sintra and rate path, BoJ rate-hike dynamics, plus direct EM Asia data prints for Indonesia, Korea and China. The key call is that markets over-read the Fed as hawkish, which has meaningful implications for the USD strength impulse that has been compressing EM carry and pressuring EM FX.

**Key points**
- Core PCE printed 32bps MoM (vs Nomura's 38bps expected), YoY ticked to 3.4% from 3.3%; the trimmed-mean PCE was only 23bps MoM, widening the gap between headline and trimmed measures to ~100bps — a split the Fed is actively debating internally.
- Nomura argues the Fed Chair's press conference was actually dovish: focused on left side of the decimal on inflation, flagged medium-term rate cuts still in the dot plot, emphasised productivity-driven disinflation; market's hawkish read is seen as premature.
- June payrolls forecast: ~70k (Nomura), headline slowing from earlier strength driven by temporary World Cup-related leisure/hospitality hiring and front-loaded local government hiring; unemployment held at 4.3%, seen edging toward 4.1% by end-2026.
- ECB removes one expected hike due to faster-than-anticipated oil pullback (US-Iran MOU signed); now expects 2 more hikes after June's 25bp. Sintra conference next week — watch for ECB speakers on services inflation persistence and second-round effects. Euro HICP forecast 3.1% YoY June, core/services seen edging up.
- BoJ: June meeting summary shows two members favour faster hikes but that is not the mainstream view; December hike remains Nomura's base case. Government's basic economic policy (due early July) likely to include language calling for accommodative stance — raising the hurdle for BoJ action. JGBs have rallied on easing oil-inflation concerns.
- China: Official PMI forecast 49.8 for both manufacturing and non-manufacturing — sub-50 contraction territory, flagging weak domestic demand and supply chain disruption.
- Korea: May IP -2.4% MoM (autos, chips); export values +61.7% YoY driven by chips; trade surplus widening. CPI 3.2% June vs 3.1% May, core stable at 2.5%. Bank of Korea expected to lower inflation forecasts in August on lower oil.
- Indonesia: June CPI expected 3.4% YoY (from 3.0%) on weather/supply disruptions; core stable at 2.57% — pass-through from headline to core is contained, reducing urgency for BI tightening.

**FX / rates implications**
- If Nomura's dovish Fed read is correct, the recent second-wave USD/DXY rally is overdone; a softer June payrolls (~70k) would reinforce that view and provide relief to EM FX broadly — watch USDINR, USDMXN and USDBRL for reversal setups.
- Indonesia's contained core CPI supports BI on hold; IDR supported by benign inflation backdrop but vulnerable to any USD rebound.
- Korea's chip-export surge and widening trade surplus are KRW-positive structurally; BoK's August forecast downgrade on oil is incrementally dovish for rates, supportive of duration.
- China sub-50 PMI is a headwind for CNH and regional risk sentiment, particularly for MYR, KRW and commodity-linked EM.
- ECB retaining two more hikes is EUR-supportive at the margin; EUR strength historically correlates with softer DXY, a positive read-through for EM carry.
- BoJ dovish government pressure and December-only hike base case keeps JPY carry-fund dynamics intact — supportive for high-yielding EM carry (INR, IDR, MXN) as long as risk sentiment holds.
- Oil drop (Iran MOU) is disinflationary for oil-importing EM Asia (India, Philippines, Indonesia, Korea) — reduces pressure on current accounts and central banks, modestly bullish for INR, PHP, IDR.

**Watch list**
- June US payrolls (next Friday): Nomura ~70k; sub-100k would validate dovish Fed re-read and weaken USD — key risk event for EM FX broadly.
- ECB Sintra conference speeches (next week): any shift in ECB rate-hike guidance or commentary on second-round inflation effects moves EUR and risk tone.
- China official PMI (Monday): 49.8 base case; a miss below 49.5 would pressure CNH and regional risk-off.
- Bank of Korea August meeting: watch for inflation forecast downgrade confirming BoK on hold/dovish, supporting KRW duration.
- Indonesia June CPI release: 3.4% expected; a surprise above 3.5% would test BI patience.
- Japan government basic economic policy statement (early July): language on BoJ accommodation could soften JPY and alter carry dynamics.
- US-Iran MOU implementation: continued progress would keep Brent under pressure, extending disinflation tailwind for EM Asia oil importers.

episodes.title: Global FX: Mid-Year Outlook pushbacks, payrolls, CNY, GBP
episodes.summary:
**Summary**
JP Morgan's FX Strategy team defends their mid-year thesis: bullish USD and bullish non-dollar-funded EM carry. EUR/USD fair value ~1.11 implies dollar has catch-up room despite rates repricing; US exceptionalism via labor market, AI, and growth differentials gives dollar bulls multiple paths beyond just Fed hikes.

**Key points**
- EUR/USD target 1.10, USD/JPY mid-160s; fair value gap at ~1.11 means FX has not absorbed the rates repricing (~40bp of Fed hikes priced)
- Core PCE 6-month avg 0.34% MoM vs Fed's required 0.22%; JPM unemployment forecast 4.1% vs Fed SEP 4.3% — labor market tighter than Fed projects
- September earliest live Fed hike; July unlikely; payrolls next week key — unemployment rate and wages the arbiters, not headline print
- Carry preferred via non-dollar funders (EUR, CHF, SEK, JPY, KIW) against high-yielders; dollar vs high-yielders not the trade
- CNY/CNH: PBOC fixing briefly above 6.82 triggered profit-taking on longs; $65-70bn dividend outflow season peaks in ~2 weeks; medium-term BOP (export conversion, foreign bond/equity buying, tighter outflow regs, PBOC ahead of US-China summit) keeps bullish trend intact; fixing spread narrowing toward 100-150 pips could re-attract fresh buyers
- European composite PMI in contraction for first time in 2 years — reinforces US growth differential, adds weight on cyclical SEK
- EUR/NOK fair value 1140; Norges Bank ~15bp August hike priced; Norway CPI in 2 weeks
- GBP constructive vs low yielders; EUR/GBP 0.8620 break watched; Burnham speech next week is UK fiscal risk premium catalyst

**FX / rates implications**
- EM carry (BRL, MXN, INR, IDR vs EUR/CHF/SEK) is the preferred expression — non-dollar funding avoids the USD headwind while capturing yield
- CNY near-term drift higher (dividend season); once washout clears, fixing spread compression re-opens bullish trend — watch for CNH to outperform on BOP dynamics
- Lower oil reduces hike odds for non-US central banks (dovish read for BI, BSP, BoK margins) but not the Fed — net USD positive, EM divergence likely
- European PMI contraction is an added negative for EUR and EUR-correlated assets, reinforcing CHF and EUR as preferred carry funders

**Watch list**
- US payrolls (next week): unemployment rate vs Fed SEP 4.3%; wage growth vs unit labor cost cycle lows
- Core PCE run rate vs 0.22% MoM Fed requirement
- PBOC daily fixing spread to spot — narrowing to <150 pips signals CNY trend resumption
- Norway CPI (~2 weeks): Norges Bank August hike trigger; EUR/NOK 1140 fair value
- EUR/GBP 0.8620 technical break; Burnham speech for UK fiscal rule flexibility detail

episodes.title: The Rebasement Trade
episodes.summary:
**Summary**
Brent Donnelly and Alfonso Peccatiello push back hard on the hawkish Fed repricing under new chair Worsh, arguing the rates options distribution is dangerously skewed toward 3–6 hikes that will never materialize — a tail-that-won't-deliver setup with direct read-through to USD, gold, and EM carry. They close with specific country calls on Colombia, Brazil, Mexico, South Africa, and Hungary, making this one of the more actionable EM FX pods in recent weeks.

**Key points**
- July hike probability (~30–40%) called "insane" — 12/12 FOMC voters held in June; macro backdrop (GDP, unemployment, claims) is essentially unchanged from Feb 2026 when 2 cuts were priced; oil flat, 1y inflation breakevens now lower than pre-war levels
- New Fed chair "performative hawkishness" paralleled to Powell's Oct 2018 "long way from neutral" — a credibility signal, not a policy commitment; ECB and BoE already backing off, and CBs tend to move as a pack
- Options distribution has the probability of 6 hikes equal to the probability of 1 cut; any 6-hike scenario requires a 50bp emergency-style move; hosts see these tails not delivering as the macro alpha opportunity
- Hormuz closure (4 months) produced near-zero commodity impact: oil essentially unchanged, fertilizer and corn fully retraced; inflation swap/oil correlation means oil weakness directly drives breakevens lower, removing the second-round effects argument for Worsh hawkishness
- USD positive carry resembles 2012–2019 regime — long USD is both a carry and a portfolio hedge; that dual property naturally attracts flows and keeps the trend sticky near term
- Gold euphoria index at lowest-ever reading; skew inverted (downside protection being bid); negative carry on gold/silver worsening as USD forward rates rise; needs catalyst — July FOMC or weak data — before the fade pays
- EM positioning against USD described as "cleansed"; dollar puts burning off but not being replaced; month-end corporate dollar buying creating a feedback loop into further USD strength near term
- Colombia (COP): top pick — new right-wing government frees BanRep to hike ~100bp that was politically blocked pre-election; massive carry + capital flows unwinding prior risk premium; USDCOP already at lows, meaning entry is clean
- South Africa (ZAR): fixed 3% inflation target (no band), SARB hawkish, fiscal deficit aggressively narrowing, credit rating upgrades underway — "everything investors want to see"
- Hungary (HUF): €20bn+ EU fund unlocking + euro accession optionality driving capital inflows; MNB cutting to resist HUF strength likely to fail (SNB analogy); multi-instrument play in rates and FX
- Brazil (BRL): Lula leading Bolsonaro on Polymarket by a widening margin; carry still captured via options but straight directional positioning cautious on political backdrop
- Mexico (MXN): USMCA renegotiation risk keeping longs nervous; approaching 200-day MA which could be attractive entry on a dollar turn

**FX / rates implications**
- USD/DXY: Momentum intact and carry-supported but increasingly overcooked; catalyst-dependent fade — July FOMC dovish surprise (war ending, oil lower, "wait and see" language) could swing September pricing from 90% hike to 50%, flipping the carry narrative quickly
- COP: Best risk/reward in EM — carry, capital flow tailwind, and a central bank now free to act; USDCOP at lows is an unusually clean entry
- MXN: Near 200-day MA; high fuel for re-entry once dollar reversal catalyst materialises, but USMCA headline risk limits conviction near term
- ZAR: Structural re-rating story; fiscal credibility + hawkish SARB + credit upgrades = carry with improving sovereign risk backdrop
- HUF: Capital flow appreciation likely to persist despite MNB resistance; rates and FX both valid expressions
- BRL: Carry via options preferred over directional given Lula election trajectory and idiosyncratic fiscal uncertainty
- UST 2y: Already flagged as long by Donnelly; July FOMC is the timing catalyst for a rally if Powell/Worsh pivots to data-dependent hold language
- Gold: Contrarian long setup building (euphoria trough, inverted skew) but requires USD/rates catalyst; not yet actionable

**Watch list**
- July FOMC: Key catalyst — watch for any softening in Worsh language on hike urgency; could flip September odds from ~90% to ~50% and trigger USD/gold reversal
- Oil price: Real-time inflation expectations proxy; further weakness removes remaining second-round effects justification for Fed hikes
- BanRep (Colombia): Timing of 100bp hiking restart; watch rate decision dates and governor commentary post-election
- Brazil election polls / Polymarket: Lula gap vs Bolsonaro/Renán Santos — widening gap = BRL carry trade more comfortable via options
- USMCA renegotiation headlines: Binary risk for MXN positioning
- SARB credit watch: Rating agency actions on South Africa sovereign; fiscal rule legislation timeline
- Month-end USD demand: Corporate buying flows into June 30 could extend dollar strength 24–48h before reversal setup improves

episodes.title: AI's macro boost, a hawkish Fed and Burnham's balancing act
episodes.summary:
**Summary**
Capital Economics flags a hawkish Fed divergence trade: 75bp of hikes forecast (3×25bp) versus ~40bp currently priced by the market, keeping USD structurally bid. The AI investment boom bifurcates EM Asia—Korea and Taiwan outperform on hardware demand—while an equity-driven risk-off scenario is the explicit EM tail risk, with currencies and sovereign spreads the first casualties.

**Key points**
- US core PCE at 3.4% YoY / 0.32% MoM in May; Capital Economics sees only a slow decline and forecasts the Fed hikes 75bp total (3×25bp) vs market's ~40bp; Kevin Warsh's debut FOMC already delivered a hawkish shock partially absorbed by market
- US Q2 GDP nowcast tracking ~2.5% QoQ annualised; AI investment drives roughly one-third of US growth; stripping it would take growth to mid-to-high 1s—weak by US standards but still above Europe
- China activity proxy: ~3% YoY; two-speed economy with domestic consumption weak but exports broad-based and strong; EU pushback on trade surplus flagged as a tail risk
- US June payrolls due Thursday (unusual timing): CE forecasts 110k, partly World Cup and July 4th leisure & hospitality hiring; unemployment seen ~4.3%
- BoE divergence: Capital Economics forecasts no hikes and cuts to 3% by end-2027 vs market pricing end-2027 rates at 4%; GBP seen weakening vs USD on this gap

**FX / rates implications**
- Fed 75bp gap versus 40bp market pricing is a structural USD/DXY upside catalyst; higher UST yields compress EM carry and weigh on capital flows across Asia and LATAM broadly
- KRW and TWD are relative EM Asia outperformers on AI hardware exposure but remain vulnerable to a risk-off turn driven by AI equity froth dissipating
- China ~3% growth and domestic weakness cap CNY/CNH upside; broad export strength limits sharp depreciation but EU tariff escalation is a tail to monitor
- Risk-off scenario (AI equity selloff distinct from real-economy slowdown): broad EM FX weakness and sovereign spread widening; semiconductor equity volatility is the leading indicator
- BoE/Fed divergence reinforces GBP/USD downside; UK gilt sensitivity to Burnham fiscal loosening adds a second leg of GBP risk

**Watch list**
- Thursday US June payrolls: CE forecasts 110k; beat = USD strength and EM headwind; miss = temporary relief rally
- Core PCE next monthly print for confirmation of Fed hike trajectory
- Semiconductor and hyperscaler equity volatility as real-time proxy for AI boom durability
- BoE MPC vote count at July and September meetings for hike risk crystallising
